entry.title: Clarification on deprecation of SAP GUI transactions KB21N and KB23N in SAP S/4HANA 2025
entry.content:
Hi

We're using SAP S/4HANA 2022 On Premise and planning to upgrade to 2025. An Early Watch Report indicates that the KB21N and KB23N Apps are 'Deprecated since SAP S/4HANA 2025'.

OSS Note 3266933 confirms deprecation of Related apps in SAP S/4HANA 2023.

The SAP S/4HANA 2025 'What's New' documentation states that these apps have been replaced by Manage Direct Activity Allocation (F3697).

I need clarification on whether the term 'Apps' refers only to the Fiori apps KB21N, KB23N, KB24N or if the SAP GUI transactions KB21N and KB23N are also deprecated.

Currently, I use SAP GUI transactions KB21N and KB23N, and these are being flagged in the Early Watch Report.  We don't actually use the Apps KB21N or KB23N.

Also, I want to know if the drilldown functionality in CJI3N of Labour bookings (via CATS) will still drilldown to KB23N in SAP S/4HANA 2025.

Thanks

S

entry.title: Re: Partial settlement in condition contract management
entry.content: Hi Kai, Please check your Pricing procedure(Settlement document) configurations. Check if Standard accruals group condition is configured. Check accruals reversal condition type is listed at condition contract type settings.

entry.title: Re: HOW TO TRANSFER THE MATERIAL FROM COST CENTER (K) TO...
entry.content: You cannot directly “move” stock from a cost center (account assignment K) back into inventory, because the goods receipt with account assignment K is treated as consumption and not valuated stock. Once posted, it updates expense accounts, not inventory. The SAP‑recommended way is to reverse the consumption posting and re‑receive into inventory, or use a special movement type like 202 or 411 depending on your business rules.

entry.title: Crystal Reports - Multiple Parameters in SAP B1 Web Client
entry.content:
Hi experts,

Is there any limitation for multiple parameters in SAP Business One - Web Client? Just in case, we are running FP2602 (MSSQL).

I've created a report with two parameters: JobCode and CurrencyOption. In the Desktop client, the report runs smoothly prompting all parameters set, whereas in the Web client it does not show the Currency parameter (which was the last one to come in) and throws an error when running: "An error has occurred while generating the PDF file".

Inspecting the browser, I see that CurrencyOption is being passed as null (as expected - there's no prompt to enter it)

Is this a WC limitation or is there a different way to implement multiple parameters here?

I'd appreciate your thoughts.

Best regards
Felipe

entry.title: Re: BADI SD_SLS_MODIFY_ITEM_REQDATE not triggered when th...
entry.content:
Hi @wolfgang_lindner2 ,
Could you please explain why you need an additional call of the BAdI after the delivery block is removed on header level? Does the requested delivery date depend on the date when the delivery block is removed? If yes, how do you want to determine that date? 

In general, the BAdI SD_SLS_MODIFY_ITEM_REQDATE is only called if the Item is processed completely which is not the case if the delivery block is removed.

There are two potential solutions:

1. Raise a feature request via Customer Influence Portal  and request the "Delivery Block" to be added to the "Modification of Sales Document in Finalize Phase before Save" BAdI. With that, you could define that all items get reprocessed in case the Delivery Block has been changed on header level.


2. Use "Modification of Custom Fields in Sales Documents at End of Finalize Phase Before Saving" BAdI to determine the change of the Delivery Block (compare current Delivery Block with the one on the database which you could get via select on I_SalesDocument)  and do a dummy change to the item which should be reprocessed. This will reprocess the item and the BAdI SD_SLS_MODIFY_ITEM_REQDATE will be called. Disadvantage of that approach is, that you only see the result after saving the sales document.

best regards,
Christian

entry.title: Re: How to create Business Partner Master Data to S4 Hana...
entry.content:
From the screenshots, it appears you are looking at SAP Extended Withholding Tax (EWT) calculation details during billing/invoice posting. The key point is that the "Tax on Sales/Purchases Amount" shown there is not necessarily the full VAT amount from SD pricing. It is the amount that SAP is subtracting from the withholding tax base according to the withholding tax configuration.
The Withholding Tax (WHT) is being calculated on the Net Amount shown in the analysis screen, not on the amount you expected.

Gross Amount = 230,777.12
Tax on Sales/Purchases Amount = 4,816.79
Net Amount = 225,960.33
WHT = 225,960.33 × 20% = 45,192.07 ,which matches the posted WHT amount.

The reason the Tax on Sales/Purchases Amount is only 4,816.79 (instead of the full 12% VAT of 24,726.12) is most likely due to withholding tax configuration. SAP is not deducting the full VAT from the WHT base; it is using a configured tax amount/formula to determine the taxable base.

entry.title: Sales order Header and Item Delivery Block should be behavior should be do same
entry.content:
We have a requirement to align the behavior of the Sales Order Header Delivery Block and the Schedule Line Delivery Block.

Currently, the system behavior is different in both scenarios:

  1. When the delivery block is applied at the schedule line/item level, the confirmed quantity is set to 0, and the sales order goes into incomplete status.

  2. When the delivery block is applied at the header level, the confirmed quantity is also expected to become 0. However, after saving the order, the document is still shown as complete.

The expectation is that both the Header Delivery Block and Schedule Line Delivery Block should behave in the same way. If the confirmed quantity is set to 0 due to the delivery block, then the sales order should also be marked as incomplete, similar to the schedule line/item level behavior.

entry.title: How SAP MDG lets you download and fix problem records directly from your process
entry.content:
Introduction

If you have ever run a mass change or consolidation process in SAP Master Data Governance, you know how valuable the validation step is. However, when the validation step stops due to errors, the erroneous records are stuck inside the system. Error messages are visible, but there was no way to extract, fix, and re-upload them.

And when a file upload fails due to invalid source IDs, all you get was a generic message like "Load contains invalid source IDs: 2". No details, no way to tell which records are actually affected. Either way, users end up rebuilding files manually.

This pain point was also one of the most strongly requested topics in SAP Customer Influence, with many customers highlighting the need for a smoother way to handle failed records. This was addressed, both following the same principle: giving a possibility to download a file of the problematic records, in exactly the format needed to fix and re-upload.

Feature 1: Export Process Data with Messages in the Validation Step

The Problem

In a consolidation or mass change process, the validation step checks the record correctness. When errors are found, the process stops and shows aggregated KPIs (e.g., "47 records with errors"). You can drill into the Details UI to see which records failed and why, but there was no way to export that subset for offline correction and re-upload.

What Was Delivered

The details UI of the validation step directly includes a new export action. When pressing the button it navigates to the export app and the filter for selected records is automatically set.

What it does:

  • Exports all records currently displayed in the validation step's overview tab
  • Includes the related messages (errors, warnings) as an additional sheet/section in the exported file
  • Uses the standard MDG export file format, the exported file can be edited offline and re-uploaded directly into a new import or mass processing run

After downloading the file errors and warnings are shown in the ! Messages ! tab of your excel sheet:

Availability

  • MDG on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2022
  • SAP S/4HANA cloud public edition

Feature 2: Download Invalid Records During Upload

The Problem

When uploading a file for a mass process, the system performs a technical validation, e.g. it checks whether the IDs in the file exist in the system. If they do not, it raises a generic error:

"Load contains invalid source IDs: 3"

That is all the users got, just generic information, no more details. Which three records? Which IDs? Users had to manually inspect the file, compare it with the system, and figure it out themselves.

What Was Delivered

Whenever invalid records in a file are detected, it is possible now to export those records from the original file by an “Export”-button in File Import Log area.

The file is in the same format as the upload, so users can correct the records (fix or remove the invalid IDs) and re-upload without rebuilding the file from scratch.

Availability

  • MDG on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2023 in classic mode
  • MDG on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025 in cloud-ready mode
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Why These Two Features Matter Together

Both features address the same need: when something goes wrong in an MDG process, users need a way to act quickly. With both features, you can download the problematic records as a file, fix them offline, and use that file as the starting point for a follow-up process.

Feature 1 helps when errors are found during the validation step. Feature 2 helps even earlier, when the file upload itself fails due to invalid IDs. In both cases, the result is the same: a downloadable file with exactly the records that need to be fixed. This means users no longer must rebuild files from scratch or investigate manually which records caused the error. This saves time, reduces the load on technical teams, and makes the whole process more reliable, especially when working with large data volumes where even a small error rate can mean hundreds of records to fix.

Have you ever used one of these features?

_________________

Further Links and Resources

  • SAP Master Data Governance community https://pages.community.sap.com/topics/master-data-governance
  • SAP Help Portal https://help.sap.com/docs/MASTER_DATA_GOVERNANCE - Various help documents for e.g. MDG on S/4HANA Cloud Private and Cloud Public Edition
  • SAP Influence Portal https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#campaign/3497 - SAP Master Data Governance on SAP S/4HANA: Submit new ideas or vote on existing requests to shape the MDG roadmap
  • SAP Learning https://learning.sap.com/courses/sap-master-data-governance-on-sap-s-4hana - Learning content covering MDG strategy, domains and processes related to MDG central governance, consolidation and mass processing end-to-end

Scenario:

Employee makes a change on 15th July with effective date 1st August.

I need to understand how to handle two cases:

Case 1 — Fetch twice:
Pick up the record on 15th July (when changed) AND again on 1st August (when it becomes effective).

Case 2 — Fetch only on effective date:
Pick up the record only on 1st August when it actually becomes effective — ignore the 15th July change entirely.

What I want to understand:

  1. What is the correct OData filter condition in the CPI SuccessFactors adapter for both cases?
  2. What is the correct Compound Employee API approach for both cases?
  3. Does SF OData API support filtering by EffectiveStartDate for entities like EmpJob, EmpEmployment, etc.?

Would really appreciate if someone can shed light on the expected behavior and the right filter approach for both scenarios.

entry.title: The SAP Fiscal Calendar, End to End: How It Gets Populated, How Data Loads in BW, and Where the ABAP
entry.content:
Every FI posting, every CO report and every BW query leans on the fiscal period — yet very few of us ever stop to trace where that period actually comes from and how it travels through the landscape. There is no "fiscal calendar load" job anywhere. So how does the fiscal calendar get populated, how does it function through every data load, and where does ABAP actually run in all of this? The short answer: it is born as configuration in ECC, derived onto every document at posting time, physically copied into BW as global settings, stamped onto every record BW extracts — and for the time characteristics themselves, served virtually by ABAP classes with no master data load at all. Let's walk through each stage.

Stage 1 — It starts as configuration, not data (OB29)

Fiscal calendars in ECC are controlled by the Fiscal Year Variant, configured via transaction OB29 and used across FI, CO and MM. Nobody uploads fiscal calendar data — the moment a variant is defined and its periods maintained in OB29, the system populates a small family of Customizing tables behind the scenes:

Table

Purpose

T009

Fiscal year variant (header) — variant ID, number of posting periods, year-dependent flag, calendar year flag

T009B

Period dates per fiscal year variant — start/end date for each posting period (and special periods)

T009C

Assignment of company code shortened fiscal year (used when a fiscal year is shortened, e.g., mid-year go-live)

T009T

Fiscal year variant texts (descriptions)

T009Y

Fiscal year variant — year-dependent version (if the calendar-year-dependent flag is set, periods can differ by year)

 

Two variants you will meet on almost every project: K4 (calendar year — 12 posting periods plus 4 special periods, where period 001 = January) and V3 (April to March — the standard variant for India and several other countries, where period 001 = April). K4 barely needs T009B at all because periods equal calendar months; V3 is where the real mechanics of the table come into play.

The year shift — the small entry that makes non-calendar fiscal years work

For a variant like V3, each month entry in T009B carries not only a period number but also a year shift (annual displacement) of -1, 0 or +1. This is the entry that tells the system which fiscal year a calendar month belongs to. Under V3, April through December carry shift 0 — a posting on 15.05.2026 lands in period 002 of fiscal year 2026. But January through March carry shift -1 — a posting on 15.01.2027 lands in period 010 of fiscal year 2026, because it still belongs to the fiscal year that started in April 2026. This one column is the entire secret behind non-calendar fiscal years, and it is exactly what downstream systems consume rather than recalculate.

One neighbouring concept worth separating out: the fiscal calendar is not the factory calendar (table TFACS, maintained via SCAL). The factory calendar defines working days and holidays for logistics and scheduling; the fiscal calendar defines posting periods for accounting. Both travel to BW as global settings, but they answer completely different questions.

Stage 2 — How the calendar reaches documents (assignment and derivation)

Configuration alone doesn't put a fiscal period on anything. The variant first has to be assigned, and then the period is derived at posting time.

Table / Field

Context

T001-PERIV

Company code's assigned fiscal year variant

CSKS / CEPC etc.

Controlling objects inherit the fiscal year variant from the company code

BKPF-MONAT / GJAHR

Posting period and fiscal year on FI documents, derived using T009B logic

 

The derivation itself is simple: given a posting date plus the fiscal year variant, the system looks up T009B (applying the year shift) to determine which period the date falls into — periods 1 to 12, plus special periods 13 to 16 for adjustments and audit. This is done via standard function modules such as FIRST_DAY_IN_PERIOD_GET, LAST_DAY_IN_PERIOD_GET and DATE_TO_PERIOD_CONVERT. The result (MONAT, GJAHR) is stamped onto the document. Special periods are never derived from a date — periods 13 to 16 are entered manually by the user during year-end closing, which is why a posting date in March can sit in period 013.

In other words, the fiscal period on a document is not stored calendar data — it is computed once at posting time from the T009B rules and then persisted on the document itself. This detail is exactly what makes the BW side behave the way it does.

Stage 3 — How the fiscal calendar physically reaches BW: Transfer Global Settings

Here is the part most people miss. BW does not query ECC's T009 tables remotely — it holds its own local copy of them. When a source system is connected, the administrator runs Transfer Global Settings (in RSA1, right-click the source system). This function copies the source system's global Customizing — fiscal year variants (T009/T009B), currencies (TCUR*), units of measure (T006*) and factory calendars — into identically structured tables inside the BW system itself.

So the fiscal calendar is populated in BW not through a DataSource or an InfoProvider, but as replicated Customizing sitting quietly in BW's own T009/T009B. BW never uses these tables as reporting data — it uses them as the rulebook for time conversion and for query runtime logic, which we'll see below. This is also why the transfer has to be repeated whenever the variant configuration changes in the source: BW's copy does not update itself.

Stage 4 — How the data load actually works in BW

With the rulebook in place, let's follow a transaction record end to end and see where fiscal time gets attached.

Step 1: Extraction — the data arrives already stamped

Business Content extractors in FI/CO (for example the General Ledger line-item extractors) read the documents where MONAT and GJAHR were already stamped at posting time. So the DataSource delivers fields like FISCVARNT and FISCPER pre-populated by ECC — the extractor is simply forwarding the result of the T009B derivation that happened when the document was posted. Nothing fiscal is computed during extraction.

Step 2: Staging and transformation — the classic load path

The record then follows the standard BW flow: DataSource → InfoPackage into the PSA → Transformation → DTP → target (DSO/ADSO or InfoCube). The transformation is where the source fields are mapped to the Time InfoObjects — FISCVARNT to 0FISCVARNT, FISCPER to 0FISCPER, the posting date to 0CALDAY, and so on.

Step 3: Automatic time conversion — where BW's local T009B earns its keep

Time characteristics enjoy a special privilege in BW transformations: automatic time conversion and derivation. If the source supplies only a date (0CALDAY) plus the fiscal year variant, BW can derive 0FISCPER, 0FISCPER3 and 0FISCYEAR automatically — and it performs that derivation using its local copy of T009B, transferred in Stage 3. The same mechanism derives 0CALMONTH, 0CALQUARTER and 0CALYEAR from 0CALDAY. This is precisely why a stale Transfer Global Settings shows up as wrong fiscal periods in BW: the derivation is only as correct as the local rulebook.

Step 4: Storage — where fiscal time finally lives in BW

Once loaded, the values settle into BW's own structures. In InfoCubes, each cube's time dimension table stores 0FISCPER, 0CALDAY and friends as dimension values, linked through SID tables; in DSOs/ADSOs they are simply fields on the record. If fiscal period hierarchies are built, they are stored like any hierarchy (e.g., /BI0/HFISCPER). From this point on, fiscal period is just another characteristic value — with one defined mapping into calendar dates, used mainly for display, sorting and hierarchy purposes.

InfoObject

Purpose

0FISCVARNT

Fiscal year variant (carried over from ECC's T009)

0FISCPER

Fiscal year/period combined (e.g., "2026001")

0FISCPER3

Fiscal period alone (3-char, e.g., "001")

0FISCYEAR

Fiscal year alone

0CALDAY

Calendar day

0CALMONTH

Calendar month (YYYYMM)

0CALWEEK

Calendar week

0CALQUARTER

Calendar quarter

0CALYEAR

Calendar year

 

Two modelling details worth knowing here. First, 0FISCPER is compounded to 0FISCVARNT — a period value like "001" is meaningless on its own, because period 001 is January under K4 but April under V3. The compounding forces every fiscal period value to carry its variant, which is why 0FISCVARNT must always be present (mapped or set as a constant) whenever 0FISCPER is filled in a transformation. Second, 0FISCPER is stored internally in the format YYYYPPP (e.g., 2026001) but displayed in external format (e.g., 001.2026) — a detail that regularly surprises people writing routines or filters against the raw value.

Where does ABAP actually run during a BW load?

A common mental model is "BW has classes, and data loads from those ABAP classes." The reality is more precise, and worth getting right: the load is driven by BW objects — DataSources, InfoPackages, Transformations, DTPs — and ABAP classes are the machinery that BW generates or ships to execute some of them. The classes are the engine room, not the source of the data.

On the extraction side, the code is mostly function modules, not classes: Business Content extractors run extraction function modules through the Service API (SAPI), and generic extractors built in RSO2 are based on a table/view, an InfoSet or a function module.

On the transformation side, classes genuinely appear — but they are generated by the system. When a transformation is activated, BW generates an ABAP program containing a local class, and every routine you write — start routine, end routine, field routine, expert routine — becomes a method of that generated class, working on parameters like SOURCE_PACKAGE and RESULT_PACKAGE. You write the method body; BW generates and owns the class around it. The DTP then executes this generated program at load time. Underneath all of it, the DTP framework, request handling and ADSO activation are implemented in SAP standard classes (the CL_RS* family) — framework code you never touch.

And a note for newer stacks: in BW/4HANA, a transformation without ABAP routines can be HANA-optimized — pushed down and executed entirely inside HANA as a calculation scenario, with no ABAP class running during the load at all. This is exactly why avoiding ABAP routines is a standard performance recommendation in that generation.

Why time characteristics have no master data load — virtual master data read classes

Open 0FISCYEAR in InfoObject maintenance and look at the Master data/texts tab, and you'll find the most elegant piece of this whole story. The setting Master Data Access is not "Default" — it is "Own Implementation", pointing to a master data read class: CL_RSMD_RS_0FISCYEAR.

"Default" would mean the InfoObject has physical master data tables (/BI0/P*, /BI0/T*) filled by data loads. "Own Implementation" means the opposite: the InfoObject has no stored master data at all. Whenever anyone needs its values — a query run, an F4 value help, a text display — the system calls this class at runtime and the values are generated on the fly. There is no DTP, no PSA, and no master data table filling up with a row for every year that has ever existed.

Why did SAP design it this way? Because fiscal years and periods are rule-based, not data-based. The set of valid fiscal years is trivially derivable, and fiscal periods are fully determined by the T009/T009B rules that BW already holds locally via Transfer Global Settings. Loading them as master data would mean materializing an infinite, rule-generated list. So SAP ships the time characteristics — 0FISCYEAR, 0FISCPER, 0CALDAY, 0CALMONTH and friends, each with its own CL_RSMD_RS_* class — with virtual master data access instead. The calendar was never data to begin with, and here that principle is implemented literally in code.

The distinction to keep sharp: transaction data still loads exactly as described in Stage 4 — the value "2026" arrives stamped on every record. It is only the master data of the time characteristics (the list of valid values, their texts, their ordering) that is never loaded, because the read class serves it virtually. Compare this with 0MATERIAL or 0CUSTOMER, whose master data is genuinely business-defined and must be loaded from the source via attribute and text DataSources. Time characteristics are the special case where SAP replaced the load with a class.

Stage 5 — What happens at query runtime

The local T009B copy and the read classes come together at query runtime. The OLAP engine consults them whenever fiscal logic is needed: deriving the current fiscal period from today's date for a variable, calculating period offsets (previous period, same period last year), building year-to-date ranges, serving F4 value helps for fiscal years and periods, and sorting periods correctly — including keeping special periods 013–016 in their proper place after period 012. None of this would work if BW treated 0FISCPER as a plain text value; it works because BW knows, from its replicated T009B and its time-characteristic read classes, how periods relate to dates and to each other.

The distinction that matters: source of truth vs consumer

Putting the stages together, the population story becomes very clear. ECC is the single source of truth: the fiscal calendar is populated there once, as Customizing in OB29, and applied millions of times, as period derivation on every posting. BW's fiscal calendar is populated twice over — once as replicated Customizing via Transfer Global Settings (the rulebook), and continuously as already-stamped values on every extracted record (the data). Its time characteristics need no master data load at all, because their read classes generate values from the rules. At no point does BW independently recalculate what ECC decided.

This has a practical consequence: if the variant configuration changes in ECC — a new period arrangement, a shortened fiscal year via T009C, a newly maintained year for a year-dependent variant — BW does not pick it up on its own. The global settings must be re-transferred, and only records loaded afterwards reflect the new rules. So when fiscal periods in BW look wrong, check in this order: was the period maintained in OB29 for the new year, were global settings re-transferred, and is the transformation mapping or deriving the time characteristics as expected. It is almost always one of these three — not a calculation error in BW.

Aspect

ECC

BW

Master config table

T009 (variant), T009B (period dates), maintained via OB29

Local replicated copy of T009/T009B via Transfer Global Settings — used for derivation, never for reporting

Characteristic / field

PERIV, MONAT, GJAHR

0FISCVARNT, 0FISCPER, 0FISCYEAR

Master data load

Not applicable — configuration, not data

None for time characteristics — served virtually by CL_RSMD_RS_* read classes ("Own Implementation")

Role

Source of truth — derives the period at posting time

Consumer — stores fiscal period as a reporting characteristic and derives only via the replicated rulebook

Special periods

Periods 13–16 (adjustment periods) supported in T009B, entered manually at closing

0FISCPER supports adjustment periods as additional period values, sorted after period 012

 

A note on newer stacks

In newer generations — BW/4HANA, or embedded analytics on S/4HANA — the same fiscal calendar is exposed through CDS views such as I_FiscalYearVariant and I_FiscalYearPeriod, rather than through the raw pool tables. The underlying logic is still the familiar T009-based configuration; only the access layer has been modernized.

Wrapping up

So the next time someone asks "where does the fiscal period actually come from, and how does it work in BW?", the answer fits in a few lines: it is born as Customizing in OB29 (T009/T009B, with the year shift doing the heavy lifting for non-calendar years), derived onto every document at posting time, copied into BW as global settings, delivered pre-stamped on every extracted record, re-derived in transformations only when needed — always against BW's local rulebook — and served virtually at query runtime by master data read classes that generate fiscal values from rules instead of loading them. There is no fiscal calendar "load" because the calendar was never data to begin with — it is configuration that every downstream layer consumes, and in BW that principle goes so deep that even the master data is a class, not a table.

If you found this useful, I'd be glad to hear how fiscal year variants are handled in your landscape — especially anyone dealing with year-dependent variants or shortened fiscal years, where the population story gets more interesting.

entry.title: Re: Reimagining Certification for the Way We Work Today
entry.content:
@srinivasan_selvam8f27f5 
To answer your questions:

1. Every certifcation is structured differently and users could have varied experiences as well. If the first question is a cross questions then this is the expected flow of the certification. We cannot inform you how many cross question will be in a certifcation, as this is not an information we share to learners prior to their ceritfcation. But for most of our scenario based certifcation, kindly expect 20-25 questions in total that will be asked for the entire certifcation
2. Your exam is unproctored and time boxed, and within Yoodli you can take a pause to do some thinking before you provide your answers. In cases of concern or need for clarifaction, we recommed using SAP Joule for consultant
3. For every question asked in your scenrio based certifcation we recommed spending 1-3 mins asnwering each question.
4. We recommend not doing this unless absolutely necessary, if you need a break, you can simply pause the exam and resume as long as you are within the time box. Do remember than pausing the roleplay has no effect on the tme box allocated for that exam, so be cautious while pausing.

entry.title: Massenänderung von Glossarverknüpfungen in Prozessen
entry.content:
Guten Tag zusammen,

folgende Ausgangssituation haben wir. Wir haben Attribute angelegt, welche sich auf eine Glossarkategorie beziehen. Wir wollen aktuell das Glossar aufräumen und nicht mehr benötigte Einträge entfernen, bei dem entfernen sollen auch die Einträge in den Prozessschritten entfernt werden. Gibt es eine Möglichkeit dies per Massenpflege zu machen, aktuell gehe ich ihn jeden Prozess rein und wähle den/die Prozesschritte aus und entferne den Eintrag manuell.

Das mag bei einem Prozess noch recht zügig gehen, bei 40 Prozessen und jeweils mehreren Prozesschritten, dauert das natürlich entsprechend lange.

Besten Dank für die Unterstützung.

Viele Grüße
Christoph

entry.title: New Course: Integrating SAP Enterprise Service Management
entry.content:
Hi everyone, 

We are super excited and pleased to announce the release of our new Integrating SAP Enterprise Service Management course on learning.sap.com

 

🧩 When should I take this course?

This intermediate course should be taken upon completion of the pre-requisite courses: 

  • Implementing SAP Enterprise Service Management 

For those who are new to Enterprise Service Management, we would also recommend to go through the Learning Journey:

  • Implementing SAP Service Cloud Version 2

⭐ Why Should You Take This Course?

This course is designed to help consultants take their knowledge of SAP Enterprise Service Management to the next level by configuring and implementing the required integration to execute end to end scenarios. 

  • Gain practical ESM expertise — Build hands-on knowledge to implement, configure, and integrate SAP Enterprise Service Management, equipping you to extend ESM principles beyond IT into business functions such as HR, finance, procurement, and customer service.
  • Unlock integration capabilities — Learn how to connect ESM with SAP Integration Suite and SAP S/4HANA and/or SAP Successfactors, enabling you to streamline operations and accelerate deployment across your organization's SAP landscape.
  • It's free and role-relevant — The course is available at no cost and is designed specifically for Administrators, Consultants, and Business Users who need to configure self-service features, build forms, and manage business documents within ESM — helping you deliver real customer value and operational efficiency.

🎯 Learning Objectives

Upon completing this course, you will be able to:

  • Understand how integration contributes towards efficient resolution in SAP Enterprise Service Management
  • Advantages of integration and key considerations when starting an implementation project 
  • Successfully setup and integrate business documents and forms 
  • Setup and integrate SAP Enterprise Service Management to SAP S/4 HANA and SAP Successfactors
  • Identify integration opportunities, explain the necessary components, and recognize the configuration steps to perform integrations between ESM, SAP Integration Suite, and S/4HANA Public Cloud.

📘 Course Structure

This course is structured into 3 units and structured in a way to methodically take you through the typical integration setup during a project. 

🚀 Get Started Today!

Ready to take your knowledge of SAP Enterprise Service Management to the next level?

Enroll in our latest course, Integrating SAP Enterprise Service Management, today and start your learning journey!

You can find the course here: Integrating SAP Enterprise Service Management

📢 Spread the Word!

Help us share this exciting update with partners, colleagues, and other stakeholders.

📝 We Value Your Feedback

After completing the course, please take a moment to share your feedback. Your insights help us continuously improve the learning experience.

entry.title: Business Data Architecture: Artifact Roles - Controlled Execution Units
entry.content:
From transformations to platform implementation

In the previous post, we defined transformations as explicit, classified changes that specify what logic belongs where - which modeling layers and data product types each transformation can execute in. Transformations make architectural boundaries enforceable.

But transformations are logical definitions, not platform implementations. When you implement transformations using platform objects, a critical challenge emerges:

Scenario: The troubleshooting nightmare

It's month-end closing. Revenue numbers don't match between the executive dashboard and detailed report. You open the Revenue Data Product and find ten artifacts with technical names like "VW_REVENUE_CALC", "TF_METRICS_PROC", "TBL_REV_STAGING". Which contains the error? The names give technical hints but reveal nothing about purpose. You spend thirty minutes reading SQL across multiple objects just to understand the data flow. The clock is ticking.

This is the core problem: every analytics platform provides technical objects - SAP Datasphere has Views and Transformation Flows, Snowflake has Views and Stored Procedures, Databricks has Notebooks and Delta Tables. But the technical object type tells you the technology, not what it's meant to do. The purpose is completely implicit.

Without explicit purpose, business logic leaks into objects meant for simple composition, data products bypass controlled access points, and when something breaks you waste critical time reading implementations just to understand what each object does.

Introducing artifact roles

The missing piece is explicit purpose labeling. The solution is to define artifacts not by their technology, but by their role in the architecture.

With artifact roles, that same Revenue Data Product would be immediately readable: two Source artifacts (bringing in data), two Transformation artifacts (one for Currency Conversion, one for Revenue Recognition), and one Wrapper artifact (the controlled access point). You'd know instantly that the error has to be in one of the two Transformation artifacts - the business logic layer. Two objects to check instead of ten. Problem found in five minutes instead of thirty.

 

An artifact role has a clearly defined purpose and a set of enforceable constraints. When you see an artifact labeled with a specific role, you immediately know two things: what it does, and what it's not allowed to do. This clarity accelerates delivery - developers immediately understand where logic belongs, reducing rework and eliminating time spent deciphering hidden logic across solutions.

Artifact Roles Explained

The architecture defines artifact roles that organize into five functional categories. This categorization reflects how data products are built - from data movement through logic execution to storage and orchestration:

  • Data Boundaries (Source, Wrapper) - Control how data enters and exits data products
  • Data Transformation (Transformation, Combine) - Define how data changes within data products
  • Business Semantics (Dimension, Semantic Model) - Provide business context and analytical structure
  • Data Materialization (Persist, Buffer) - Manage data materialization and availability
  • Workflow Orchestration (Orchestration) - Coordinate when and how artifacts run

Let's explore each role in detail, understanding its purpose, constraints, and platform implementations:

 

Platform implementation guide

The table below translates each artifact role into the specific platform objects developers create. This is your implementation reference: when building a Source artifact in Snowflake, use External Table, Table, or View. When building a Transformation artifact in Databricks, use Notebook or Lakeflow Pipeline.

Use this mapping to move from architectural concepts to actual implementation - ensuring consistent structure across platforms while leveraging each platform's native capabilities. This table is designed for daily reference as you build data products.

Note: Some platform objects serve dual roles - Databricks Lakeflow Pipeline can function as Transformation (defining a single data flow) or Orchestration (coordinating dependencies across artifacts).

 

Why consistent artifact roles matter across platforms

Organizations running multi-platform data architectures - SAP Datasphere alongside Snowflake, Databricks, or Microsoft Fabric - face a fragmentation problem. Without consistent artifact roles, each platform develops its own vocabulary and patterns. Teams spend energy translating between platforms instead of building solutions.

When artifact roles are consistent across platforms, architectural boundaries remain enforceable regardless of technology. A Transformation artifact in SAP Datasphere has the same purpose, constraints, and position in the data flow as a Transformation artifact in Snowflake. The platform objects differ (Graphical View vs. SQL View), but the role and its rules are identical.

This consistency delivers strategic advantages:

  • Predictable cross-platform composition: When a Snowflake Analytical Data Product consumes a Datasphere Metric Data Product, both teams know the connection happens via Wrapper artifacts. The pattern is the same regardless of platform direction.
  • Unified governance: Architects review data products across all platforms using the same criteria - are Transformation artifacts executing only allowed patterns? Are Combine artifacts free of business logic? The rules don't change by platform.
  • Platform independence: Organizations can adopt new platforms or migrate between them without redefining their architectural principles. The roles remain constant even as technology choices evolve.

Without this consistency, multi-platform organizations fragment into platform silos where each team operates under different rules. With it, platforms remain distinct in implementation but unified in architecture.

Platform-specific optimization within role boundaries

While artifact roles remain consistent across platforms, each platform optimizes implementation using native capabilities. Snowflake excels at SQL-based transformations, Databricks at Python/ML workloads, Fabric at Power BI integration. The roles define WHAT each artifact does; platforms choose HOW based on their strengths.

What artifact roles deliver

Role-based artifacts transform how analytics teams work day-to-day:

  • Common language across teams: Developers across different teams and platforms immediately understand each other's work. A Transformation artifact means the same thing whether you're in the Datasphere team or the Snowflake team - no translation required.
  • Instant understanding: Source artifacts bring data in, Transformation artifacts execute business logic, Combine artifacts join results. Roles make purpose visible without reading implementation code.
  • Easier to maintain others' work: When you inherit a data product built by someone else, role-based structure means you immediately understand what each artifact does and where to make changes. No time wasted reverse-engineering someone else's approach.
  • Enforced boundaries: Data products must connect via Wrapper artifacts. Internal artifact access is prohibited by definition - violations are immediately visible in code review.

Up next: Data Product Patterns

You now understand artifact roles - the nine building blocks with explicit purposes and constraints. But one critical question remains: How do you ensure artifacts are combined correctly every time a data product is built?

When building a Revenue Metric Data Product, which artifacts are required? Which are optional? In what sequence should transformations execute? Should currency conversion happen before or after revenue recognition logic?

That's where Data Product Patterns come in - composition blueprints that define how artifacts combine to build complete, compliant data products. Patterns ensure every Revenue Metric Data Product is structured the same way, with transformations executing in validated sequences that maintain data integrity.

This blog post is part of the Business Data Architecture for Modern Analytics Landscapes series.

⬅️ Previous Post | Next Post ➡️

entry.title: Insight into how SmartRecruiters may work for SuccessFactors Internal hires
entry.content:
In this article we will discuss how the SmartRecruiters integration roadmap implies how internal hires will work for SuccessFactors Internal hires.  There are many different types of internal hires.

As of July 15th, Internal hires from SmartRecruiters will not be sent from SmartRecruiters to SuccessFactors.  However, there is now an indication of how this will work in the roadmap. The roadmap states:

‘An internal hire flag is passed to EC/Onboarding and surfaced in MPH to distinguish from external hires and handle them appropriately in EC. EC remains the source for creating or adjusting the internal move / future-dated action (e.g. job/position change) on the employee record; that part is not driven automatically from SR.’

This implies that all internal hires will be sent to MPH and once we update the Employee Central record, we can trigger a crossboarding process in Onboarding.  This is NOT vastly different to what happens at the moment.  However, this is only 1 type of internal hire.  There are 5 key types of internal hires:

1. Employee moves with the same country and legal entity.

This is covered in the example above; their Employee Central record is updated first and then we trigger onboarding process if required.  Normally Onboarding is not triggered as most of their data remains the same, the only updates are to job and compensation.  In my opinion based on SmartRecruiters roadmap in Q3 this is not an issue.

2. Employee applies for a job in another country and transfers to that country.

In this example the employee needs to terminate their current employment and be rehired into the new country.  With SuccessFactors recruitment the employee is sent to Onboarding and a  ‘rehire on new employment’ task is created in Onboarding, once this is completed onboarding is started and once this is completed they will land in MPH.  

Sending them into first MPH will not work as we will need new data from the employee, for example the address in the new country, new social security number etc.  In addition Employee Central may have lots of rules forcing them to have this data before you hire them out of MPH.  Even if you can bypass the rules the candidate will be in Employee Central with incomplete data and payroll replication may fail.   Based on what we know in Q3 this is a gap with SmartRecruiters integration.

3. Employee is currently employed as a contingent worker and applied for a permanent position.

In this example the contingent worker can’t just be flipped to an employee in Employee Central.  They need to have their contingent worker record terminated and rehired into the permanent role.  This is very similar to point 2.  Sending them into first MPH will not work. The new record will need new data,  for example, the address, a social security number, a DOB attributes that are missing on a CW record.  As a result we will have an incorrect employee record and this could cause payroll replication to fail.  Based on what we know in Q3 this is a gap with SmartRecruiters.

4. Candidate applies for a role and is sent on a Global Assignment

The current processes with SuccessFactors recruitment send it to MPH and once the Global Assignment is added in Employee Central it triggers an onboarding process.  What we need to ensure is that the employment is marked as a Global Assignment and not just an internal hire.  We need to ensure SmartRecruiters can distinguish those on a Global Assignment.

5. Candidate applies for a role and is sent on a Concurrent Employment

The current processes with SuccessFactors recruitment send it to MPH and once the Concurrent Employment is added in Employee Central it triggers an onboarding process.  As with Global Assignments we need to ensure that the hire is marked as a Concurrent Employment and not just an internal hire.  We need to ensure SmartRecruiters can distinguish those on a Concurrent Employment.

Whilst the roadmap give some indication of how it will work there are still many unanswered questions.  Once i know more i will update this blog.

entry.title: SAP CPI SuccessFactors Adapter — Filter condition to handle future-dated effective records
entry.content:
This is something that has been bugging me for a long time but I never got a clear answer. Since I am currently not working on a SuccessFactors project I cannot test this myself — so reaching out to the community to understand the behavior.

Scenario:

Employee makes a change on 15th July with effective date 1st August.

I need to understand how to handle two cases:

Case 1 — Fetch twice:
Pick up the record on 15th July (when changed) AND again on 1st August (when it becomes effective).

Case 2 — Fetch only on effective date:
Pick up the record only on 1st August when it actually becomes effective — ignore the 15th July change entirely.

What I want to understand:

  1. What is the correct OData filter condition in the CPI SuccessFactors adapter for both cases?
  2. What is the correct Compound Employee API approach for both cases?
  3. Does SF OData API support filtering by effectiveStartDate for entities like EmpJob, EmpEmployment etc.?

Would really appreciate if someone can shed light on the expected behavior and the right filter approach for both scenarios.

#SuccessFactors #CompoundEmployee #SAP Cloud Integration

entry.title: Re: Clarification on Using BW DataSources in Datasphere M...
entry.content:
Hi Radhakrushna, 

yes, HCM and CDS views is a gap. HCM extractors are a bit more complex. FI/CO depends. The question is rather from life-cycle/architecture perspective. Will you replace your HCM or FI/CO system in the near future? 

For HCM -> yes, BW Datasource would be the solution for now, go for ODP. Easy and working well with Init/Delta if Replication Flow is used. ABAP connection. 

For FI/CO -> depends. ACDOCA and compatibility views in S/4 allow to migrate quite a lot 1:1. 0EC_PCA_1 is however not really valid example :). But will you replace your FI/CO ECC with S/4? And if yes, S/4 PCE, or on-prem? 

And do not forget custom development, user-exits in extractors. These may be challenging to implement into CDS views. 

 

I would go for existing extractors. Migrate the reporting to DSP / SAC and have the users up and running there in DSP/SAC. Usually migrations of ECCs are postponed anyway, so that leaves time to migrate the extraction part later. Dependent on the new system (HCM to SF f.e. will not help to have CDS views, but FI/CO to S/4 would). 

Cheers, Martin

entry.title: Enable Sorting of Outer Dimension by Measure in Planning Tables Without Blocking Input Readiness
entry.content:
Hi team,

In SAC tables with two nested dimensions on the same axis (e.g., Customer as outer dimension, Version as inner dimension with a fixed logical order), it is currently not possible to sort the outer dimension by a measure value while (a) preserving the inner dimension's fixed block order, and (b) keeping the table input-ready for planning.

For example, Customer 2 should be on the top as it has the highest quantities:

  • "Value Sorting" only works on the innermost dimension(?); it cannot be initiated on the outer dimension.
  • "Rank" can achieve the visual result (sorting Customer blocks while preserving Version order), but it applies a filter/limit mechanism to the whole axis, which disables planning input for the entire table — even when N is set high enough that no rows are actually excluded.

Did I miss something? Could anyone help?

Thanks and Best regards,
Simon

entry.title: Re: Sales order Header and Item Delivery Block should be...
entry.content:
That is how SAP works. Header Delivery Block will only prevent creation of deliveries and no impact on ATP. Review whether your business requirement is about visibility (order should show incomplete) or logistics (confirmed qty must reset). If visibility is enough, incompletion procedure is sufficient. If logistics alignment is required, clean‑core extensibility is the SAP‑recommended way.
Try to convince the client to go with standard SAP functionality.

entry.title: Offboarding Review Tasks Not Generated – Process Shows Completed
entry.content:
Hi Community,

I have configured the default Offboarding process with:

  • Review Employee Information
  • Review Offboarding Information
  • Offboarding Tasks

Both standard rules are active and return true:

  • ONB2_OFB_ManagerReviewCheck
  • ONB2_OFB_EmployeeReviewCheck

However, after termination, no review task appears for either the manager or employee, and the Offboarding process immediately shows Completed.

The manager assignment, employee user, permissions, and process variant have already been checked.

Has anyone faced this issue or identified any additional configuration that could cause all review steps to be skipped?

entry.title: Introducing Smarter Automation: AI Agent Skills for SAP Automation Pilot
entry.content:
We are excited to announce a new set of AI agent skills for SAP Automation Pilot, now available as an open-source repository on GitHub. Built for Claude Code, these skills bring generative AI directly into your automation development workflow, making it faster and more intuitive to build, review, debug, and manage automations on SAP BTP.

Whether you are just getting started with SAP Automation Pilot or have been working with it for years, these skills remove a significant amount of the manual effort involved in day-to-day automation development. The goal is simple: let you focus on what you want to automate, not on the mechanics of how to express it.

 

What We'll Cover

  • A Brief Introduction to SAP Automation Pilot
  • Where to Find It
  • How It Works
  • A Closer Look at Each Skill
  • Prerequisites and Setup
  • A Sample Prompt to Get You Started
  • Start Building Today
  • Useful Links

 

A Brief Introduction to SAP Automation Pilot

SAP Automation Pilot is SAP's built-in automation service for SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). It is built for DevOps and Operations teams who need to automate complex operational tasks - from application and database lifecycle management to incident response, health monitoring, and scheduled workflows, without writing extensive custom code.

If you are not familiar with SAP Automation Pilot yet, the official documentation is a great place to start: SAP Automation Pilot Documentation

 

Where to Find It

The full repository is publicly available on GitHub:

  • SAP Automation Pilot Agent Skills - GitHub

All ten skills are included and ready to use. The repository is open source and free to clone.

 
 

How It Works

Once set up, these skills are available directly inside Claude Code - whether you use the VS Code extension, the CLI, or the desktop app. From there, you interact with SAP Automation Pilot through natural conversation rather than manual configuration.

The skills are designed to support you across the entire automation lifecycle – building new commands, reviewing and validating your work, deploying content to your tenant, triggering and monitoring executions, investigating failures, managing schedules, and more. Rather than covering one specific area, they work together as a complete toolkit for SAP Automation Pilot development and operations.

 
 

A Closer Look at Each Skill

  • Automation Pilot Command Generation – Generate production-ready commands from a plain language description, with error handling and best practices built in.
  • Automation Pilot Catalog Explorer – Browse and search existing commands and catalogs in your tenant before building something new.
  • Automation Pilot Command Review – Validates commands, inputs, and catalogs against security requirements, naming conventions, and best practices.
  • Automation Pilot Content Management via API – Create, update, list, and remove commands and catalogs in your tenant through the Content API.
  • Automation Pilot Executions API – Trigger commands, monitor progress, retrieve logs, and control executions programmatically.
  • Automation Pilot Debugger – Pulls logs, filters failures, and matches error patterns to remediation suggestions when an execution goes wrong.
  • Automation Pilot Scheduled Executions API – Create and manage recurring automation schedules with flexible timing and full timezone support.
  • Automation Pilot MCP Server Generation – Expose your SAP Automation Pilot commands as tools for AI assistants via MCP server definitions.
  • Automation Pilot HTTP Request Executor – Guidance for configuring the HTTP executor used in most API-facing commands.
  • Automation Pilot ExecuteScript Executor – Covers script executors for Bash, Python, Node.js, and PowerShell with language-specific best practices.

 

Prerequisites and Setup

Prerequisites:

  • Claude Code (VS Code extension, CLI, or desktop app)
  • curl and jq installed on your machine
  • An SAP Automation Pilot tenant with API credentials

Setup:

  1. Clone the repository from GitHub
  2. Create a .env file with your connection details — your regional AUTOPI_HOSTNAME (emea, amer, apac, aus, or ksa), AUTOPI_USERNAME, AUTOPI_PASSWORD, and AUTOPI_DEFAULT_CATALOG
  3. Run source .env to load your environment
  4. Open Claude Code and start a conversation

 

A Sample Prompt to Get You Started

Here is an example of what you can do with the Command Generation skill right away:

"Create an SAP Automation Pilot Command that restarts a Cloud Foundry app and waits for it to become healthy, with proper error handling."

From that single prompt, Claude Code will generate a complete, production-ready SAP Automation Pilot command – correctly structured, with health check logic, error handling, and best practices already in place.

 

Start Building Today

Getting started takes only a few minutes, and the skills are ready to use from your very first conversation. Try it with your own SAP Automation Pilot tenant and see how much faster your automation development can move.

 

Useful Links

  • SAP Automation Pilot Agent Skills - GitHub
  • AI Skills Library
  • SAP Automation Pilot Documentation
  • Claude Code Documentation

entry.title: Stop Guessing. Start Simulating. The Case for Data-Driven Business Process Simulation
entry.content:
Every business transformation starts with a simple question:  what will happen if we make this change?

It's a question that organizations spend millions trying to answer. Teams build business cases, analyze KPIs, consult experts, and benchmark against peers. Yet despite all the planning, transformation initiatives often deliver unexpected results. A bottleneck shifts instead of disappearing. An automation project accelerates one activity while slowing down another. A redesign that looked perfect in workshops creates unforeseen issues once it reaches production.

The challenge isn't a lack of ideas, it's a lack of certainty.

 

From Opinion to Evidence

Traditional process simulation has existed for decades, and has been consistently underused for a simple reason: it required an enormous amount of manual work before it could produce a single meaningful result. Analysts had to build process models from scratch, estimate parameters by hand, make simplifying assumptions about resources and timing, and then hope their guesses were close enough to reality to make the output trustworthy. In most organisations, the effort required to set up a simulation exceeded the value it returned.

The new Data-driven business process simulation capability, released in beta in July 28th, changes this equation.

By combining real process execution data with advanced simulation capabilities, organizations can test change scenarios before implementation, evaluate trade-offs, and quantify business impact in a risk-free environment. Instead of making decisions based on assumptions, intuition, or incomplete information, teams can validate improvements using evidence grounded in how their processes actually operate.

Two Ways In, One Source of Truth

SAP Signavio Process Intelligence offers two entry points to data-driven business process simulation, designed to meet organisations wherever they are.

For teams with access to event log data, a fully data-driven simulation is available immediately. The engine reads the historical execution record, learns the core simulation parameters automatically, and produces a working model of how your process behaves, without requiring any manual configuration. This is a statistically grounded representation of how your process has actually been running, ready to be the baseline against which you test every proposed change.

For teams with established process models but limited execution data, the model-based entry point provides a credible simulation capability without requiring an event log. This path also replaces legacy simulation tools that relied on manual parameterisation, giving organisations a modern, maintainable simulation foundation built on the same platform as their process governance and analytics.

Both paths converge at the same place: a scenario management layer that lets you create, execute, and compare simulation scenarios via APIs, generating synthetic event logs that represent proposed future states and enabling direct comparison of as-is versus to-be KPIs.

What This Makes Possible

  • When you are considering automating an activity, simulation tells you whether the process is stable enough to automate and what the downstream impact on cycle time and resource utilisation will be, before a single bot is deployed.
  • When you are evaluating whether to add headcount to a struggling team, simulation tells you whether the bottleneck is actually a capacity problem or a routing problem, before the hiring decision is made.
  • When you are building a business case for a process redesign, simulation provides the quantified, evidence-based projections that move a conversation from opinion to decision, before the investment is committed.

This is the shift from reactive to predictive process management. Instead of implementing a change and measuring the consequences, you test the consequences before the implementation. Instead of discovering bottlenecks after go-live, you identify them in a safe environment where they cost nothing to fix. Instead of aligning stakeholders on assumptions, you align them on evidence.

The Foundation for Intelligent Process Transformation

Data-driven business process simulation does not sit alongside your process intelligence capabilities, it amplifies them. Conformance checking tells you where your process is deviating today. Root cause analysis tells you why. Simulation tells you what will happen if you fix it, and which fix produces the best outcome across cycle time, cost, and resource utilisation simultaneously.

Together, these capabilities form a closed loop: discover how processes actually run, understand why they perform the way they do, simulate the impact of changing them, implement with confidence, and monitor the result continuously. Each step in the loop is grounded in the same operational data. Each decision is supported by the same evidence base.

For organisations that have invested in process modeling, data-driven simulation completes the picture. Your designed process becomes the benchmark. Your event data becomes the calibration. And your proposed improvements get tested in a virtual environment where failure is free, before you commit them to the real one.

The organisations that transform fastest are not the ones that move quickest.

They are the ones that know what they are doing before they do it.

entry.title: MPR Score section blank for specific employee in Perf form (SF)
entry.content:
We are facing an issue in our Performance Management form (template: "Average MPR Score : BPO") where the "Average MPR Score" section renders completely empty for a specific employee, while the same section displays correctly (with the rating dropdown and item "1.1 Average MPR Score") for other employees on the exact same route map and template.

Details:

  • Form: BPO-Performance Appraisal Review 2025-26 (Supervisory_MPR)
  • Affected employee: shows "Average MPR Score (80.0%)" and "Competency (20.0%)" headers, but no rating item/dropdown appears underneath the Average MPR Score heading
  • Competency section items and ratings display and function normally
  • Route Map is identical for both affected and unaffected employees
  • Field "Average MPR Score" (Field ID: bizx-actual, Type: number) settings look consistent across the template
  • Checked Field Permissions under Edit Categories — found a Write permission block scoped to Role: "HR Rep." for Route Step "Average MPR Score"

Any guidance on where to check next (Goal Plan data, Manage Form Goal Plan Data Association, or dynamic role resolution) would be appreciated.
